description: Registry of optional codebase-analysis backends. STATIC metadata only (no source, no install). CodeBase Analyzer reads this to render the guided-install menu at point of need; live availability is resolved per backend via its validator. 'blessed' = vendored stub + validator, preferred. 'candidate' = clone/audit-only or user-level install, gitignored, opt-in. Nothing here is installed automatically.

storage.principle: Split by artifact, do NOT blanket-ignore. Three classes: (1) installed tool, (2) heavy regenerable index = local + gitignored, (3) shareable summary = COMMITTED so a team builds once and everyone pulls the latest without rebuilding/re-enriching.
storage.installed_backend: The tool's source/binary. Clone-build backends -> AI-Dev-Shop/integrations/<tool>/upstream/ (gitignored via integrations/*/upstream/). uv/pipx backends (code-review-graph, graphify CLI, codebase-memory-mcp binary) install user-level (~/.local), outside the repo. NOT in ADS-memory. Reinstallable via the validator, so losing it is cheap.
storage.heavy_local_index: Large regenerable indexes/DBs/caches stay LOCAL and gitignored — never committed (GitHub hard-rejects files >100MB). Home: ADS-memory/.local-artifacts/analyzers/<tool>/<target>/ wherever the tool supports an external data dir: graphify (GRAPHIFY_OUT), codebase-memory-mcp (HOME), code-review-graph (--data-dir). Examples too big to commit: code-review-graph SQLite (~600MB), codebase-memory-mcp cache, serena LSP cache.
storage.shared_summary: Small, expensive-to-produce summaries ARE committed to a tracked folder so teammates share them without rebuilding. Prime case: understand-anything's LLM-enriched knowledge-graph.json (~2.6MB) — regenerating it costs LLM calls, so committing it saves real money/time. Size-gate: only commit artifacts comfortably under ~50MB (GitHub warns at 50MB, hard-rejects at 100MB).

shareable_artifacts.others: code-review-graph / codebase-memory-mcp / serena / codegraph produce heavy or binary local indexes — rebuild rather than commit

storage.in_target_index_note: codegraph (.codegraph/), understand-anything (.understand-anything/), and serena (.serena/) hardcode their index into the PROJECT/CODEBASE root (the root that contains the AI-Dev-Shop folder — NOT the AI-Dev-Shop or ADS-memory root) and offer no relocation flag. To centralize: relocate where a flag exists, else copy the shareable summary into the tracked shared folder after build. The analyzer offers to gitignore the heavy in-target dirs in the target repo.
storage.symlink_caveat: Git commits a symlink as a pointer (mode 120000), so it IS uploadable — but it only carries DATA to a teammate if the symlink target is ALSO tracked. A symlink into the gitignored ADS-memory tree is a DANGLING link on their checkout (verified). Absolute-path targets break across machines; Windows symlink support is unreliable. Use symlinks only for local centralization; COMMIT actual files (or copy them) for team sharing.
storage.future: Planned: a pre-push / CI hook on branch/main that rebuilds the index and regenerates+commits the shared summaries, keeping committed graphs current automatically.

recommendation_tiers.basis: real-code head-to-head on a ~2,300-file TS/Electron monorepo (github.com/nexu-io/open-design), clean-state; see the capability matrix at skills/code-navigation/reference/analyzer-capability-matrix.md
recommendation_tiers.tier_1_rationale: the lightweight, low-friction defaults: fast structural callers+impact with the smallest index (codegraph, ~3x source on disk), and natural-language/semantic search with a tiny shareable summary (understand-anything).
recommendation_tiers.tier_2_rationale: deeper or more specialized AND heavier on disk — add when you need them and mind the index size: whole-repo path/community (graphify); deepest multi-hop chains + Cypher (codebase-memory-mcp, ~6x source); strongest all-in-one architecture analytics but the most bloated index (code-review-graph, ~13x source — a 2.5k-file repo produced a ~600MB SQLite DB).
recommendation_tiers.tier_3_rationale: LSP-exact precision but heaviest to operate (a Language Server per language + interactive onboarding). Add only when symbol exactness justifies the setup cost.
recommendation_tiers.always_on: rg is the always-fresh literal/fallback baseline and is not installable (always present).
recommendation_tiers.index_size_note: Graph DBs are far larger than the source. Measured on a 2,152-file / ~45MB-source TS monorepo (open-design): codegraph ~157MB (~3x), codebase-memory-mcp ~250MB (~6x), code-review-graph ~587MB (~13x, dominated by 7 indexes over 406k denormalized string-keyed edges — not embeddings). All local-only and regenerable; plan disk accordingly.

cost.footprint: npm deps + dist/ build; SQLite index at <target>/.codegraph/codegraph.db (~150MB on a 2.5k-file repo, ~9MB on a small one)
cost.weight: light-to-moderate (lighter DB than code-review-graph, but still hundreds of MB on a large monorepo)

recommendation.good_at: The fastest, lowest-friction structural tool: exact direct callers and change-impact from a quick CLI index, no API key, and a lighter DB than code-review-graph. Best first graph backend for 'who calls this' and 'what breaks if I change it'.
use_cases: User needs the direct callers of a function (who calls X?)
User needs the blast radius / what breaks if a symbol changes
User needs a fast structural map without a heavy index build
User wants graph answers with zero API key or external calls

backends.index_location: <target>/.codegraph/ (in-target; no relocation flag — gitignore in the target repo)

requirements.semantic_extra: semantic search needs the [embeddings] extra (sentence-transformers/torch, heavy) OR a cloud embedding provider + key — gated, not bundled
requirements.notes: persistent incremental knowledge graph; runs as an MCP server (serve/stdio)

cost.privacy: 100% local for structural; semantic embeddings optional/external
cost.footprint: SQLite graph DB (can be hundreds of MB) + FTS rows; build ~1 min + optional embed pass

recommendation.tier_note: Best all-around capability, but tier 2 for its footprint: the index balloons to ~13x source (~600MB on a 2.5k-file repo).
recommendation.good_at: The strongest all-in-one: structural callers/impact PLUS architecture-health analytics out of the box — god-component/hub detection, bridge nodes, blast-radius, surprising connections. Cleanest install of the graph tools; semantic search is gated behind a heavy embeddings extra so lean on understand-anything for natural-language.
use_cases: User needs blast-radius / change-impact of a symbol or file
User needs to find god-components / hub nodes (high in/out-degree)
User needs architecture-health signals: bridge nodes, surprising connections
User wants one tool covering callers + impact + architecture with a clean install

backends.install: uv tool install (see upstream); validator planned (Phase 4)
backends.status_check: validator not yet built; verify `code-review-graph --version` runs, else treat as unavailable

requirements.llm_for_enrichment: another LLM to generate the summaries (any capable model, provider-agnostic — local or hosted)
requirements.api_key: only if the chosen LLM provider requires one
requirements.notes: tree-sitter graph + LLM enrichment; ADS wrapper scripts (build-graph/search-graph/enrich-graph-batch.mjs) are not yet tracked

cost.privacy: enrichment calls a separate LLM (provider you choose)

recommendation.good_at: Powerful for semantic search — finds the file by meaning ('What file does X?' / 'where's the feature that handles Y?') when you don't know the symbol name. Needs another LLM to generate the summaries and slightly more setup than the others, but pinpoints conceptually once enriched.
use_cases: User needs 'What file does X?' when they don't know the symbol/function name
User needs to find the feature or module that handles a concept
User is onboarding to an unfamiliar codebase and searches by intent, not keyword

backends.index_location: <target>/.understand-anything/ (in-target; gitignore in the target repo; wrapper relocation = Phase 3)
backends.status_check: validator not yet built; verify build-graph.mjs + .understand-anything/knowledge-graph.json exist, else treat as unavailable

recommendation.good_at: Strong for reachability, shortest-path, and whole-repo community/cluster reports. One-click install; slower to build on a large repo, so reach for it when you specifically need path or community analysis.
use_cases: User needs to know if any path connects A to B
User needs to prove there is NO path / find dead regions
User needs dependency communities or cluster reports for the whole repo
User needs shortest-path between two symbols

requirements.notes: persistent local knowledge graph via CLI/MCP

cost.footprint: managed checkout + built binary + local cache

recommendation.good_at: The deepest structural backend: follows transitive call chains across multiple hops and surfaces architecture/cluster structure. Most raw depth, but overlaps code-review-graph while trading a finicky Cypher query surface — so it sits in tier 2 as the max-depth option.
use_cases: User needs to trace a call chain several hops deep (not just direct callers)
User needs the architectural modules/clusters of an unfamiliar repo
User needs which modules import or depend on X across the whole codebase
User needs full change-impact before a refactor

runtime: one Language Server per language (e.g. pyright, tsserver)
requirements.notes: LSP-exact declarations/references via MCP stdio; reached through the mcp stdio probe on non-MCP hosts

cost.footprint: python env + one language server install per language + a one-time interactive project onboarding

recommendation.good_at: Highest precision: it queries the real Language Server, so references and definitions are compiler-exact — correct even with overloaded, aliased, or re-exported names where grep and AST graphs only guess. Needs more setup BECAUSE it runs a separate Language Server per language (each must be installed), does a one-time interactive project onboarding, and operates as an MCP server (a stdio probe wrapper is needed on non-MCP hosts) rather than a one-shot CLI.
use_cases: User needs exact references to an overloaded or aliased symbol where grep is ambiguous
User needs precise, compiler-accurate go-to-definition across files
User needs language-server-grade symbol resolution that AST heuristics get wrong

backends.status_check: validator not yet built; verify integrations/serena/upstream/scripts/mcp_server.py exists, else treat as unavailable
